Title: Discovery of a previously unknown biosynthetic capacity of naringenin chalcone synthase by heterologous expression of a tomato gene cluster in yeast

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Native yeast enoyl-CoA reductase activity redirects the reconstructed pathway to dihydro-coumaroyl anthranilate amide.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Partial knockout and heterologous replacement with low-activity ECRs; metabolite profiling by LC-MS/MS with chemical standards
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a pathway gene of the tomato cluster, but a decisive host factor affecting interpretation of pathway product identity. Full deletion impaired growth because TSC13 is essential for fatty acid synthesis.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Disruption impairs growth due to essential role in fatty acid synthesis.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Native yeast TSC13 converts pathway intermediate toward a reduced derivative product. 2. Partial disruption lowered product (2), while replacement with heterologous ECRs abolished (2) and revealed the true product (3). 3. TSC13 therefore acts as a host interference factor in heterologous pathway reconstruction.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Host background gene to edit or replace when reconstructing phenylpropanoid-derived amide pathways in yeast to avoid reductive side products.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Native yeast housekeeping gene; not specifically profiled
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Effects observed under p-coumaric acid and 3-HAA methyl ester feeding in engineered yeast
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specific function not tested; affects product structure in heterologous reconstruction
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Dihydro-coumaroyl anthranilate amide / coumaroyl anthranilate amide

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Can methylate 3-HAA and support production of pathway compounds in yeast, but less effectively than SlMT2.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Individual gene expression in yeast; feeding assays; LC-MS/MS and qToF-MS metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Functionally redundant with SlMT2 for initial pathway characterization, but produced substantially lower levels of compounds (1) and (2); therefore not chosen as the principal pathway methyltransferase.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SlMT1 individually supported production of compounds (1) and (2) in yeast. 2. Activity overlapped with SlMT2 but was much weaker. 3. It methylated 3-HAA in feeding assays but was not the main enzyme pursued further.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Alternative methyltransferase for pathway engineering, though inferior to SlMT2 under tested conditions.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 3-HAA and p-coumaric acid feeding assays in yeast
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Untargeted metabolomics by qToF-MS; no public accession reported

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Shows weak methylation activity toward 3-HAA in yeast but did not support the pathway compounds under the main cluster reconstruction conditions.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Individual gene expression in yeast; substrate feeding assays; LC-MS/MS and qToF-MS metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Previously described as a putative salicylic acid methyltransferase, but salicylic acid activity was not detected in the yeast assay context used here. Detected only weak 3-HAA methylation among tested substrates.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Very low productivity in the tested system.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SlMT3 did not produce the pathway compounds in the primary minimal-pathway test. 2. Feeding assays showed weak activity toward 3-HAA only. 3. Its previously suggested salicylic acid methyltransferase role was not supported in this yeast assay context.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited value for this pathway under tested conditions.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Untargeted metabolomics by qToF-MS; no public accession reported
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: 3-HAA methyl ester / no major HCAA output

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Not required for production of the identified HCAA compounds in yeast.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene omission from heterologous pathway reconstruction; metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Present in the predicted tomato gene cluster but excluded functionally from the reconstructed pathway for compounds (1) and (2)/(3).
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Endoplasmic reticulum-associated membrane (inferred for plant P450)
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SlCYP was included in the initial five-gene cluster reconstruction. 2. Removal of SlCYP did not abolish pathway product formation. 3. Therefore it is not part of the minimal essential gene set for the discovered HCAA in yeast.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be omitted when reconstructing the minimal HCAA pathway described here.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Assessed in heterologous yeast pathway context with p-coumaric acid feeding
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Untargeted metabolomics by qToF-MS; no public accession reported

Common_Genes.Applied_Species: Escherichia coli / in vitro enzyme system
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Exhibits the same previously unrecognized amide-forming secondary activity as SlCHS in vitro, suggesting the activity may be common among plant CHSs.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant expression in E. coli; purified enzyme assay in vitro; LC-MS/MS product analysis
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used as comparative validation, not the main discovery gene in the Results; no in vivo plant or yeast engineering with AtCHS was reported here.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Type III polyketide synthase; chalcone synthase family
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely similar competition between amide synthesis and canonical chalcone synthesis, though not deeply characterized here.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCHS catalyzed amide formation with 3-HAA methyl ester and p-coumaroyl-CoA in vitro. 2. It showed substrate preference patterns similar to SlCHS. 3. This supports the idea that amide synthesis may be a broader secondary function of plant CHSs.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Provides an alternative CHS scaffold for enzyme engineering toward HCAA synthesis.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Recombinantly expressed in E. coli for protein purification
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro assay conditions with supplied p-coumaroyl-CoA and 3-HAA methyl ester

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes formation of coumaroyl anthranilate amide from 3-HAA methyl ester and p-coumaroyl-CoA in heterologous yeast; also retains canonical chalcone synthase activity for naringenin chalcone biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Untargeted metabolomics by qToF-MS; no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Previously unknown secondary activity of CHS: nitrogen-carbon bond formation (amide synthesis). In yeast with intact native TSC13, product appears mainly as dihydro-coumaroyl anthranilate amide due to endogenous reduction of p-coumaroyl-CoA; knockout/replacement of TSC13 restores the oxidized true product coumaroyl anthranilate amide. Arabidopsis CHS showed similar in vitro activity, suggesting this may be a broader secondary activity in plant CHSs.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast; minimal gene set reconstruction; in vitro enzyme assay with purified recombinant protein; substrate competition assay; site-directed mutagenesis; kinetic analysis; LC-MS/MS and qToF-MS metabolite profiling; comparison to chemical standards
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for metabolic engineering of novel hydroxycinnamic acid amides by exploiting CHS catalytic promiscuity; could be combined with methyltransferase and 4CL modules, but low catalytic efficiency for amide synthesis relative to canonical activity is a key limitation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hydroxycinnamic acid amides are associated with plant defense-related specialized metabolism; specific function of the newly observed product was not established in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Yeast cultures fed p-coumaric acid; product interpretation affected by endogenous yeast TSC13 enoyl-CoA reductase activity; assays at 25–30°C
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Hydroxycinnamic acid amide branch linked to flavonoid precursor metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid methyl ester with p-coumaroyl-CoA to form an amide bond, yielding coumaroyl anthranilate amide; canonically condenses p-coumaroyl-CoA with malonyl-CoA to form naringenin chalcone.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GDP1p/ADHt; PGK1p/PHO5t; PYK1p/MFA1t; TEF1p/CYC1t (heterologous yeast expression cassettes)
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Amide synthesis competes with canonical chalcone synthesis; 3-HAA methyl ester acts as mixed-mode inhibitor of canonical activity, dominated by competitive inhibition; lower efficiency for amide synthesis than canonical activity.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Coumaroyl anthranilate amide; canonically naringenin chalcone
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Hydroxycinnamic acid amide biosynthesis; flavonoid/chalcone biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Type III polyketide synthase; chalcone synthase family; conserved catalytic triad C164-H303-N336
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SlCHS was required with SlMT2 and Sl4CL for production of the novel hydroxycinnamic acid amide in yeast. 2. Purified SlCHS directly catalyzed amide bond formation between 3-HAA methyl ester and p-coumaroyl-CoA in vitro. 3. Active-site mutagenesis showed overlap between canonical chalcone synthesis and amide synthesis mechanisms. 4. Kinetics demonstrated the amide-forming activity is a less efficient secondary activity than canonical chalcone synthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces 3-HAA methyl ester from endogenous or fed 3-HAA and enables highest HCAA production among cluster methyltransferases in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Untargeted metabolomics by qToF-MS; no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Among SlMT1/2/3, SlMT2 gave the highest 3-HAA methyl ester production and strongest support for the pathway; activity toward p-coumaric acid and p-aminobenzoic acid was detectable in feeding assays, but the central pathway role shown here is methylation of 3-HAA.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key enabling upstream step for supplying 3-HAA methyl ester in the reconstructed pathway; formal rate-limiting status not established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Individual gene expression in yeast; minimal pathway reconstruction; substrate feeding assays; LC-MS/MS and qToF-MS metabolite profiling; comparison to authentic standard
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable module to generate 3-HAA methyl ester precursor for engineered hydroxycinnamic acid amide production in microbes or plants; may be stacked with CHS and 4CL.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hydroxycinnamic acid amides are associated with specialized defense metabolism; specific function of the product was not directly tested.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Activity assessed in yeast with or without fed p-coumaric acid and with substrate feeding including 3-HAA; 72 h growth at 30°C
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: 3-HAA methyl ester precursor formation feeding hydroxycinnamic acid amide biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid to form 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid methyl ester.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GDP1p/ADHt; PGK1p/PHO5t; PYK1p/MFA1t; TEF1p/CYC1t (heterologous yeast expression cassettes)
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broader substrate acceptance may cause off-pathway methylation products in engineering contexts.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SlMT2 alone produced compound (1), identified as 3-HAA methyl ester, in yeast. 2. SlMT2 with Sl4CL and SlCHS was sufficient for minimal pathway reconstruction of the novel HCAA. 3. SlMT2 outperformed SlMT1 and SlMT3 in 3-HAA methyl ester production. 4. SlMT2 showed strongest activity toward 3-HAA among tested substrates in yeast feeding assays.

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies p-coumaroyl-CoA from fed p-coumaric acid, enabling downstream SlCHS-dependent amide production in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A supporting flavonoid-pathway gene used for heterologous reconstruction rather than a member of the predicted five-gene tomato cluster; nonetheless essential for reconstituted HCAA production in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Minimal pathway reconstruction by gene omission/combinatorial expression in yeast; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful activation module to provide hydroxycinnamoyl-CoA starter units for engineered HCAA production.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specific function of the novel product not established; hydroxycinnamic acid amides are linked to defense metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Requires fed p-coumaric acid for pathway output in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Hydroxycinnamoyl-CoA supply branch linked to flavonoid/HCAA metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GDP1p/ADHt; PGK1p/PHO5t; PYK1p/MFA1t; TEF1p/CYC1t (heterologous yeast expression cassettes)
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Dependence on precursor feeding in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Phenylpropanoid/flavonoid precursor activation; hydroxycinnamic acid amide biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Sl4CL was required, together with SlMT2 and SlCHS, for production of the amide product in yeast. 2. Removal of Sl4CL eliminated product (2)/(3), confirming p-coumaric acid-derived precursor supply is essential. 3. Sl4CL functions as the p-coumaroyl-CoA-generating entry step of the reconstructed pathway.
